title.en: Cloud Run: Serverless Containers in Production

description.en: Run any container serverlessly: request-driven autoscaling, concurrency tuning, revisions and traffic splitting, and the honest trade-offs of scale-to-zero.

story_variants.en:
## Any Container, Zero Servers

**Cloud Run** takes a container image that listens on `$PORT` and turns it into a fully managed HTTPS service: TLS, a stable URL, autoscaling from zero to thousands of instances, and per-100ms billing while requests are being served. No cluster, no node pools, no YAML beyond the service spec. It runs on the same Borg-derived infrastructure as GKE (via Knative-compatible APIs), which is why it feels like Kubernetes with the sharp edges filed off.

The core abstractions:

- **Service** — the long-lived named thing with a URL.
- **Revision** — an immutable snapshot of image + configuration. Every deploy creates one.
- **Instance** — a running copy of a revision, created and destroyed by the autoscaler.
- Also: **Cloud Run jobs** for run-to-completion batch work, and **worker pools** for pull-based, non-HTTP workloads.

## The Autoscaler's Actual Algorithm

Cloud Run scales on **concurrency**, not CPU. Each service declares a maximum concurrent requests per instance (default 80, max 1000). Roughly: instances needed = ceiling(concurrent requests / concurrency limit), bounded by `min-instances` and `max-instances`.

| Setting | Effect | Production guidance |
|---|---|---|
| concurrency | Requests one instance handles at once | 80+ for I/O-bound APIs; 1-4 for CPU-heavy or memory-hungry inference |
| min-instances | Warm floor, prevents cold starts | 1+ for latency-sensitive services (billed while idle at a reduced rate) |
| max-instances | Cost/backpressure ceiling | Always set one; protects databases from thundering herds |
| cpu-boost / cpu always-allocated | Startup speed / background work | Enable for JIT-heavy runtimes; always-on CPU for background threads |

> **Pitfall:** Concurrency 1 with a slow framework turns modest traffic into an instance explosion. 100 in-flight requests at concurrency 1 wants 100 instances; at concurrency 80 it wants 2.

**Cold starts** are the tax of scale-to-zero: pulling isn't the cost (images are streamed and cached), but runtime boot is. Mitigations, in order of effectiveness: set `min-instances >= 1`, enable startup CPU boost, trim your image and lazy imports, and pick second-generation execution environment for full Linux compatibility.

## Revisions and Traffic: Built-in Canary Releases

Because every deploy creates an immutable revision, Cloud Run gives you progressive delivery natively:

- Deploy with `--no-traffic`, smoke-test the new revision via its tagged URL.
- `gcloud run services update-traffic api --to-revisions api-00042=10` sends a 10% canary.
- Watch error rates, then promote to 100 — or route back to the old revision in one command. Rollback is instant because the old revision still exists.

> **Callout:** This revision/traffic model is the same mental shape as Vertex AI endpoint traffic splitting you'll meet later in this track — learn it once, use it twice.

## State, Scale, and the PyMasters Confession

Cloud Run instances are ephemeral and their filesystems are in-memory. The textbook posture: keep state in Cloud SQL, Firestore, Memorystore, or GCS. But 'serverless' doesn't force 'web-scale'. Fun fact: **PyMasters itself — the platform you're learning on — runs on Cloud Run** with `min-instances=1, max-instances=1` and a SQLite database on the instance, continuously replicated to Cloud Storage with Litestream. One instance means SQLite never sees a concurrent writer from another node; Litestream makes the bucket the durability story. It's a deliberate small-scale posture: a single always-warm instance, near-zero ops, and a restore path measured in seconds. Know the rules deeply enough to know when a constrained exception is sound engineering rather than an accident.

For everyone else at max-instances > 1: statelessness is not optional, because two requests from one user will land on different instances.

## Security and Networking Defaults That Matter

- **Ingress**: allow-all, internal-only, or internal + load balancer. Internal-only plus IAM is the standard posture for service-to-service APIs.
- **Invocation IAM**: `roles/run.invoker` decides who may call the service. Service A calls service B by attaching an ID token minted for its service account — no API keys.
- **Egress**: Direct VPC egress (or a serverless VPC connector) puts outbound traffic inside your VPC so you can reach private Cloud SQL IPs.
- Each service runs **as** a service account — give it the minimum roles, never the default compute account in production.

## Decision Guide

- HTTP/gRPC service in a container, spiky traffic, small team? **Cloud Run** — the default answer.
- Needs GPUs? Cloud Run offers GPU instances (great for lightweight LLM inference), but for heavy sustained serving compare with GKE or Vertex AI endpoints.
- Long-lived websockets and in-memory session affinity as a hard requirement? Consider GKE; Cloud Run supports websockets and session affinity but instances still recycle.
- Run-to-completion nightly batch? **Cloud Run jobs**, not a service with a cron hitting it.

## What You'll Build

The challenge is the autoscaler itself, miniaturized: given a concurrency limit and min/max bounds, compute instances for a series of load levels — including the clamped cases. Once you've implemented ceiling division with floors and ceilings, every Cloud Run scaling graph you ever see will read like your own code.

description.en: A service routes traffic across immutable revisions; the autoscaler manages instances per revision.

animation_sequence.code:
import math
needed = math.ceil(load / concurrency)
instances = max(min_i, min(max_i, needed))

explanation.en: Ceiling division: 81 concurrent requests at concurrency 80 needs 2 instances, not 1.
The raw demand signal is in-flight requests divided by the per-instance concurrency limit.
Clamp between min-instances (warm floor) and max-instances (cost and backpressure ceiling).

left.label: concurrency=1 for an I/O-bound API (100 rps -> ~100 instances)

right.label: concurrency=80 for an I/O-bound API (100 rps -> ~2 instances)

description.en: Concurrency is the single most cost-decisive knob on Cloud Run.

lines: Revision api-00042 deployed, serving 0% of traffic.
$ gcloud run services update-traffic api --to-revisions api-00042=10

description.en: Canary, observe, promote — rollback is one command because old revisions persist.
The life of a Cloud Run request, including the cold-start branch min-instances removes.

instruction.en: Implement the Cloud Run autoscaler decision. Config: concurrency=80, min_instances=1, max_instances=10. Write instances_for(load, concurrency, min_i, max_i) that returns ceil(load/concurrency) clamped to [min_i, max_i]. For each load in LOADS, in order, print exactly one line: 'load=<L> -> instances=<N>', appending ' (max reached)' when the unclamped need exceeds max_i, or ' (min floor)' when the unclamped need is below min_i. Print nothing else.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
import math

CONCURRENCY = 80
MIN_I = 1
MAX_I = 10
LOADS = [40, 400, 900, 0]

def instances_for(load, concurrency, min_i, max_i):
    # TODO: needed = ceil(load / concurrency); clamp to [min_i, max_i]
    pass

for load in LOADS:
    # TODO: compute needed (unclamped) and final instances
    # print 'load=<L> -> instances=<N>' plus ' (max reached)' if needed > max_i
    # or ' (min floor)' if needed < min_i
    pass
practice_challenges.expected_output:
load=40 -> instances=1
load=400 -> instances=5
load=900 -> instances=10 (max reached)
load=0 -> instances=1 (min floor)

en: math.ceil(load / concurrency) gives the unclamped need; keep it in a variable so you can compare against the bounds for the suffix.
load=0 gives needed=0 which is below min_i=1 — that's the '(min floor)' case; 900/80 ceils to 12 which exceeds 10 — '(max reached)'.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert instances_for(40, 80, 1, 10) == 1
assert instances_for(400, 80, 1, 10) == 5
assert instances_for(900, 80, 1, 10) == 10
assert instances_for(0, 80, 1, 10) == 1
assert instances_for(81, 80, 1, 10) == 2
assert instances_for(800, 80, 1, 10) == 10

q.en: What primarily drives Cloud Run's autoscaling decision?

en: In-flight request concurrency relative to the per-instance concurrency limit
Memory pressure on the node pool

q.en: What does every Cloud Run deploy create?

en: A new immutable revision that traffic can be split against

q.en: The most effective mitigation for user-facing cold-start latency is:
Why can PyMasters safely run SQLite on Cloud Run?

en: It pins min=1/max=1 so only one writer instance exists, and Litestream replicates the DB to GCS for durability
Cloud Run persists instance disks forever
GCS mounts as a POSIX filesystem with locking across instances
